name: UO2 (3% enriched)
description: Uranium dioxide at 3 wt% U-235 enrichment. Standard fuel for commercial PWRs and BWRs.
openmcCode:
import openmc

uo2_3pct = openmc.Material(name='UO2 3% enriched')
uo2_3pct.set_density('g/cm3', 10.97)
uo2_3pct.add_nuclide('U235', 0.010000)
uo2_3pct.add_nuclide('U238', 0.323333)
uo2_3pct.add_nuclide('O16', 0.666667)
notes: Theoretical density is 10.97 g/cm³; operating pellets are typically 94–96% TD (~10.31–10.53 g/cm³). Adjust density for porosity as needed.

name: UO2 (4.5% enriched)
description: Uranium dioxide at 4.5 wt% U-235 enrichment. Common in high-burnup PWR fuel assemblies.
openmcCode:
import openmc

uo2_4p5pct = openmc.Material(name='UO2 4.5% enriched')
uo2_4p5pct.set_density('g/cm3', 10.97)
uo2_4p5pct.add_nuclide('U235', 0.015000)
uo2_4p5pct.add_nuclide('U238', 0.318333)
uo2_4p5pct.add_nuclide('O16', 0.666667)
notes: Theoretical density. Adjust for pellet porosity (typically 94–96% TD).

name: UO2 (5% enriched)
description: Uranium dioxide at 5 wt% U-235 enrichment. Maximum enrichment for standard commercial fuel under current NRC licensing.
openmcCode:
import openmc

uo2_5pct = openmc.Material(name='UO2 5% enriched')
uo2_5pct.set_density('g/cm3', 10.97)
uo2_5pct.add_nuclide('U235', 0.016667)
uo2_5pct.add_nuclide('U238', 0.316666)
uo2_5pct.add_nuclide('O16', 0.666667)
notes: Theoretical density. Adjust for pellet porosity (typically 94–96% TD).

name: UO2 (19.75% HALEU)
description: Uranium dioxide at 19.75 wt% U-235, High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium. Used in advanced reactor designs and research reactors.
openmcCode:
import openmc

uo2_haleu = openmc.Material(name='UO2 19.75% HALEU')
uo2_haleu.set_density('g/cm3', 10.97)
uo2_haleu.add_nuclide('U235', 0.065833)
uo2_haleu.add_nuclide('U238', 0.267500)
uo2_haleu.add_nuclide('O16', 0.666667)
notes: 19.75% is the maximum enrichment classified as LEU (<20%). Used in microreactors, TRISO fuels, and research reactors.

name: MOX (5% Pu)
description: Mixed oxide fuel with 5 at% Pu in heavy metal. Depleted U matrix (0.25% U-235) with reactor-grade Pu vector.
openmcCode:
import openmc

mox_5pct = openmc.Material(name='MOX 5% Pu')
mox_5pct.set_density('g/cm3', 10.97)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('U235', 0.000792)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('U238', 0.315875)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('Pu238', 0.000333)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('Pu239', 0.009000)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('Pu240', 0.004000)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('Pu241', 0.002000)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('Pu242', 0.001333)
mox_5pct.add_nuclide('O16', 0.666667)
notes: Pu vector is representative of reactor-grade Pu (Pu-238/239/240/241/242 = 2/54/24/12/8 at%). Adjust Pu vector and U enrichment to match specific fuel specifications.

name: Uranium Nitride
description: Uranium mononitride fuel at 5% U-235 enrichment. High uranium density and thermal conductivity make it attractive for advanced reactors.
openmcCode:
import openmc

un_fuel = openmc.Material(name='Uranium Nitride')
un_fuel.set_density('g/cm3', 14.32)
un_fuel.add_nuclide('U235', 0.025000)
un_fuel.add_nuclide('U238', 0.475000)
un_fuel.add_nuclide('N14', 0.500000)
notes: N-15 enrichment is sometimes used to reduce parasitic (n,p) reactions on N-14. Replace N-14 with N-15 (7015.80c) if modeling N-15-enriched UN.

name: Uranium Carbide
description: Uranium monocarbide fuel at 5% U-235 enrichment. High heavy-metal density and good thermal properties for fast reactor and space reactor applications.
openmcCode:
import openmc

uc_fuel = openmc.Material(name='Uranium Carbide')
uc_fuel.set_density('g/cm3', 13.63)
uc_fuel.add_nuclide('U235', 0.025000)
uc_fuel.add_nuclide('U238', 0.475000)
uc_fuel.add_element('C', 0.500000)

name: UZrH₁.₆ (TRIGA fuel)
description: Uranium-zirconium hydride fuel used in TRIGA research reactors. 20% enriched U with Zr-H matrix providing inherent negative temperature feedback.
mcnpCode:
c UZrH1.6 TRIGA fuel (20% enriched U)
c Density: 6.0 g/cm3 (use -6.0 on cell card)
c Atoms per formula: 1 U + 1 Zr + 1.6 H = 3.6
m8    92235.80c  0.055556  $ U-235
      92238.80c  0.222222  $ U-238
      40000.80c  0.277778  $ Zr (natural)
       1001.80c  0.444444  $ H-1
mt8   h-zr.20t zr-h.20t
serpentCode:
% UZrH1.6 TRIGA fuel (20% enriched U)
mat UZrH_TRIGA -6.0 moder hzr 1001 moder zrh 40000
92235.80c  0.055556  % U-235
92238.80c  0.222222  % U-238
40000.80c  0.277778  % Zr (natural)
 1001.80c  0.444444  % H-1
therm hzr h-zr.20t
therm zrh zr-h.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

uzrh = openmc.Material(name='UZrH1.6 TRIGA fuel')
uzrh.set_density('g/cm3', 6.0)
uzrh.add_nuclide('U235', 0.055556)
uzrh.add_nuclide('U238', 0.222222)
uzrh.add_element('Zr', 0.277778)
uzrh.add_nuclide('H1', 0.444444)
uzrh.add_s_alpha_beta('c_H_in_ZrH')
uzrh.add_s_alpha_beta('c_Zr_in_ZrH')
notes: U content is typically 8.5–12 wt% of the total fuel meat. Density and H/Zr ratio vary by fuel element type. The ZrH matrix provides a large prompt negative temperature coefficient.

name: Zircaloy-2
description: Zirconium alloy cladding used primarily in BWRs. Low neutron absorption cross section with good corrosion resistance.
mcnpCode:
c Zircaloy-2
c Density: 6.56 g/cm3 (use -6.56 on cell card)
c Weight fractions (nominal composition)
m9    40000.80c -0.9823  $ Zr (balance)
      50000.80c -0.0150  $ Sn
      26000.80c -0.0012  $ Fe
      24000.80c -0.0010  $ Cr
      28000.80c -0.0005  $ Ni
openmcCode:
import openmc

zirc2 = openmc.Material(name='Zircaloy-2')
zirc2.set_density('g/cm3', 6.56)
zirc2.add_element('Zr', 0.9823, percent_type='wo')
zirc2.add_element('Sn', 0.0150, percent_type='wo')
zirc2.add_element('Fe', 0.0012, percent_type='wo')
zirc2.add_element('Cr', 0.0010, percent_type='wo')
zirc2.add_element('Ni', 0.0005, percent_type='wo')
notes: Nominal midrange composition. Zircaloy-2 contains Ni (unlike Zircaloy-4), making it less suited for PWR water chemistry but standard for BWR fuel cladding.

name: Zircaloy-4
description: Zirconium alloy cladding used primarily in PWRs. No nickel content, which improves hydrogen pickup resistance in PWR coolant chemistry.
mcnpCode:
c Zircaloy-4
c Density: 6.56 g/cm3 (use -6.56 on cell card)
c Weight fractions (nominal composition)
m10   40000.80c -0.9819  $ Zr (balance)
      50000.80c -0.0150  $ Sn
      26000.80c -0.0021  $ Fe
      24000.80c -0.0010  $ Cr
serpentCode:
% Zircaloy-4 (weight fractions)
mat Zircaloy4 -6.56
40000.80c -0.9819  % Zr (balance)
50000.80c -0.0150  % Sn
26000.80c -0.0021  % Fe
24000.80c -0.0010  % Cr
openmcCode:
import openmc

zirc4 = openmc.Material(name='Zircaloy-4')
zirc4.set_density('g/cm3', 6.56)
zirc4.add_element('Zr', 0.9819, percent_type='wo')
zirc4.add_element('Sn', 0.0150, percent_type='wo')
zirc4.add_element('Fe', 0.0021, percent_type='wo')
zirc4.add_element('Cr', 0.0010, percent_type='wo')
notes: Nominal midrange composition. Standard PWR fuel cladding and structural material. No Ni content distinguishes it from Zircaloy-2.

name: Stainless Steel 304
description: Austenitic stainless steel widely used for reactor structural components, piping, and vessel internals.
mcnpCode:
c Stainless Steel 304
c Density: 7.94 g/cm3 (use -7.94 on cell card)
c Weight fractions (nominal composition)
m11   26000.80c -0.6917  $ Fe (balance)
      24000.80c -0.1900  $ Cr
      28000.80c -0.0925  $ Ni
      25055.80c -0.0200  $ Mn
      14000.80c -0.0050  $ Si
       6000.80c -0.0008  $ C
openmcCode:
import openmc

ss304 = openmc.Material(name='SS304')
ss304.set_density('g/cm3', 7.94)
ss304.add_element('Fe', 0.6917, percent_type='wo')
ss304.add_element('Cr', 0.1900, percent_type='wo')
ss304.add_element('Ni', 0.0925, percent_type='wo')
ss304.add_element('Mn', 0.0200, percent_type='wo')
ss304.add_element('Si', 0.0050, percent_type='wo')
ss304.add_element('C', 0.0008, percent_type='wo')

name: Stainless Steel 316
description: Molybdenum-bearing austenitic stainless steel with improved corrosion resistance. Used in fast reactor cladding, PWR internals, and hot-leg piping.
mcnpCode:

c Density: 7.99 g/cm3 (use -7.99 on cell card)

m12   26000.80c -0.6567  $ Fe (balance)

      28000.80c -0.1200  $ Ni
      42000.80c -0.0250  $ Mo
      25055.80c -0.0200  $ Mn
      14000.80c -0.0075  $ Si

openmcCode:

ss316 = openmc.Material(name='SS316')
ss316.set_density('g/cm3', 7.99)
ss316.add_element('Fe', 0.6567, percent_type='wo')
ss316.add_element('Cr', 0.1700, percent_type='wo')
ss316.add_element('Ni', 0.1200, percent_type='wo')
ss316.add_element('Mo', 0.0250, percent_type='wo')

name: Inconel 718
description: Nickel-chromium superalloy used in reactor vessel head penetrations, springs, and high-temperature structural components.
openmcCode:
import openmc

inconel718 = openmc.Material(name='Inconel 718')
inconel718.set_density('g/cm3', 8.19)
inconel718.add_element('Ni', 0.5250, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Cr', 0.1900, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Fe', 0.1852, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Nb', 0.0500, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Mo', 0.0300, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Ti', 0.0090, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Al', 0.0050, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Mn', 0.0035, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('Si', 0.0018, percent_type='wo')
inconel718.add_element('C', 0.0005, percent_type='wo')

name: Hastelloy-N
description: Nickel-molybdenum alloy developed for molten salt compatibility. Primary structural material for the MSRE and proposed MSR designs.
mcnpCode:
c Hastelloy-N (INOR-8)
c Density: 8.86 g/cm3 (use -8.86 on cell card)
c Weight fractions (nominal composition)
m14   28000.80c -0.7100  $ Ni (balance)
      42000.80c -0.1600  $ Mo
      24000.80c -0.0700  $ Cr
      26000.80c -0.0500  $ Fe
      14000.80c -0.0050  $ Si
      25055.80c -0.0050  $ Mn
openmcCode:
import openmc

hastelloy_n = openmc.Material(name='Hastelloy-N')
hastelloy_n.set_density('g/cm3', 8.86)
hastelloy_n.add_element('Ni', 0.7100, percent_type='wo')
hastelloy_n.add_element('Mo', 0.1600, percent_type='wo')
hastelloy_n.add_element('Cr', 0.0700, percent_type='wo')
hastelloy_n.add_element('Fe', 0.0500, percent_type='wo')
hastelloy_n.add_element('Si', 0.0050, percent_type='wo')
hastelloy_n.add_element('Mn', 0.0050, percent_type='wo')
notes: Also known as INOR-8 or Alloy N. Developed at ORNL for molten fluoride salt service. Modified Hastelloy-N with Ti additions improves irradiation resistance.

name: Carbon Steel
description: Plain carbon steel used for reactor pressure vessel construction (with SS cladding), structural supports, and containment liner.
mcnpCode:
c Carbon Steel (mild)
c Density: 7.82 g/cm3 (use -7.82 on cell card)
c Weight fractions
m15   26000.80c -0.9900  $ Fe (balance)
       6000.80c -0.0050  $ C
      25055.80c -0.0050  $ Mn
serpentCode:
% Carbon Steel (weight fractions)
mat CarbonSteel -7.82
26000.80c -0.9900  % Fe (balance)
 6000.80c -0.0050  % C
25055.80c -0.0050  % Mn
openmcCode:
import openmc

carbon_steel = openmc.Material(name='Carbon Steel')
carbon_steel.set_density('g/cm3', 7.82)
carbon_steel.add_element('Fe', 0.9900, percent_type='wo')
carbon_steel.add_element('C', 0.0050, percent_type='wo')
carbon_steel.add_element('Mn', 0.0050, percent_type='wo')
notes: Simplified composition for ASTM A516 Grade 70. RPV steels (e.g., SA-508) have more complex compositions with Ni, Mo, and Cr additions.

name: Light Water
description: Light water at room temperature (~20°C). Primary moderator and coolant in PWRs and BWRs.
mcnpCode:
c Light Water (H2O)
c Density: 0.998 g/cm3 (use -0.998 on cell card)
m16    1001.80c  0.666667  $ H-1
       8016.80c  0.333333  $ O-16
mt16  lwtr.20t
serpentCode:
% Light Water (H2O)
mat light_water -0.998 moder lwtr 1001
1001.80c  0.666667  % H-1
8016.80c  0.333333  % O-16
therm lwtr lwtr.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

light_water = openmc.Material(name='Light Water')
light_water.set_density('g/cm3', 0.998)
light_water.add_nuclide('H1', 0.666667)
light_water.add_nuclide('O16', 0.333333)
light_water.add_s_alpha_beta('c_H_in_H2O')
notes: Density at 20°C and 1 atm. Adjust density for operating temperature: ~0.72 g/cm³ at 300°C (PWR hot leg), ~0.74 g/cm³ at 286°C (BWR).

name: Heavy Water
description: Deuterium oxide at room temperature. Moderator and coolant in CANDU and other heavy-water reactors, providing superior neutron economy.
mcnpCode:
c Heavy Water (D2O)
c Density: 1.105 g/cm3 (use -1.105 on cell card)
m17    1002.80c  0.666667  $ H-2 (deuterium)
       8016.80c  0.333333  $ O-16
mt17  hwtr.20t
serpentCode:
% Heavy Water (D2O)
mat heavy_water -1.105 moder hwtr 1002
1002.80c  0.666667  % H-2 (deuterium)
8016.80c  0.333333  % O-16
therm hwtr hwtr.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

heavy_water = openmc.Material(name='Heavy Water')
heavy_water.set_density('g/cm3', 1.105)
heavy_water.add_nuclide('H2', 0.666667)
heavy_water.add_nuclide('O16', 0.333333)
heavy_water.add_s_alpha_beta('c_D_in_D2O')
notes: Density at 20°C. Real heavy water typically has 99.75–99.80 at% D; trace H-1 can be added if modeling isotopic purity.

name: Graphite
description: Nuclear-grade graphite moderator. Used in gas-cooled reactors (AGR, HTGR), RBMK, and molten salt reactors.
mcnpCode:
c Nuclear Graphite
c Density: 1.70 g/cm3 (use -1.70 on cell card)
m18    6000.80c  1.000000  $ C (natural)
mt18  grph.20t
serpentCode:
% Nuclear Graphite
mat graphite -1.70 moder grph 6000
6000.80c  1.000000  % C (natural)
therm grph grph.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

graphite = openmc.Material(name='Graphite')
graphite.set_density('g/cm3', 1.70)
graphite.add_element('C', 1.0)
graphite.add_s_alpha_beta('c_Graphite')
notes: Density varies 1.6–1.8 g/cm³ depending on grade and irradiation history. Nuclear-grade graphite is purified to <5 ppm boron-equivalent impurities.

name: Beryllium
description: Beryllium metal used as a neutron reflector and moderator in research and test reactors. Excellent neutron economy due to (n,2n) reactions.
mcnpCode:
c Beryllium Metal
c Density: 1.85 g/cm3 (use -1.85 on cell card)
m19    4009.80c  1.000000  $ Be-9
mt19  be-met.20t
serpentCode:
% Beryllium Metal
mat beryllium -1.85 moder be_met 4009
4009.80c  1.000000  % Be-9
therm be_met be-met.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

beryllium = openmc.Material(name='Beryllium')
beryllium.set_density('g/cm3', 1.85)
beryllium.add_nuclide('Be9', 1.0)
beryllium.add_s_alpha_beta('c_Be')
notes: Be is toxic; handling requires special safety precautions. The (n,2n) reaction on Be-9 makes it an effective neutron multiplier.

name: Beryllium Oxide
description: Beryllium oxide (beryllia) used as a moderator and reflector in compact reactors. Higher density than graphite with excellent thermal conductivity.
mcnpCode:
c Beryllium Oxide (BeO)
c Density: 3.01 g/cm3 (use -3.01 on cell card)
m20    4009.80c  0.500000  $ Be-9
       8016.80c  0.500000  $ O-16
mt20  be-o.20t o-be.20t
serpentCode:
% Beryllium Oxide (BeO)
mat BeO -3.01 moder beo_be 4009 moder beo_o 8016
4009.80c  0.500000  % Be-9
8016.80c  0.500000  % O-16
therm beo_be be-o.20t
therm beo_o o-be.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

beo = openmc.Material(name='Beryllium Oxide')
beo.set_density('g/cm3', 3.01)
beo.add_nuclide('Be9', 0.500000)
beo.add_nuclide('O16', 0.500000)
beo.add_s_alpha_beta('c_Be_in_BeO')
beo.add_s_alpha_beta('c_O_in_BeO')

name: FLiBe
description: Lithium fluoride–beryllium fluoride molten salt (2LiF-BeF₂). Primary coolant/fuel carrier for fluoride-salt-cooled and molten salt reactors. Li-7 enriched to 99.995% to minimize tritium production.
mcnpCode:
c FLiBe (Li2BeF4) - Li-7 enriched to 99.995%
c Density: 1.94 g/cm3 (use -1.94 on cell card)
c Atom fractions for 2 Li + 1 Be + 4 F = 7 atoms
m21    3006.80c  0.000014  $ Li-6
       3007.80c  0.285700  $ Li-7
       4009.80c  0.142857  $ Be-9
       9019.80c  0.571429  $ F-19
openmcCode:
import openmc

flibe = openmc.Material(name='FLiBe')
flibe.set_density('g/cm3', 1.94)
flibe.add_nuclide('Li6', 0.000014)
flibe.add_nuclide('Li7', 0.285700)
flibe.add_nuclide('Be9', 0.142857)
flibe.add_nuclide('F19', 0.571429)
notes: Li-7 enrichment to 99.995% is critical for MSR designs to limit tritium production from Li-6(n,α)T. Density varies with temperature: roughly ρ = 2.413 − 4.88×10⁻⁴·T(K) g/cm³.

name: FLiNaK
description: Lithium fluoride–sodium fluoride–potassium fluoride eutectic salt (46.5-11.5-42.0 mol%). Used as a secondary coolant and heat-transfer fluid in MSR designs.
openmcCode:
import openmc

flinak = openmc.Material(name='FLiNaK')
flinak.set_density('g/cm3', 2.09)
flinak.add_nuclide('Li6', 0.017647)
flinak.add_nuclide('Li7', 0.214853)
flinak.add_nuclide('Na23', 0.057500)
flinak.add_element('K', 0.210000)
flinak.add_nuclide('F19', 0.500000)
notes: Eutectic melting point ~454°C. Unlike FLiBe, FLiNaK typically uses natural Li since it serves as a secondary (non-fuel-bearing) coolant where tritium production is less critical.

name: Sodium
description: Liquid sodium coolant at ~100°C. Primary coolant in sodium-cooled fast reactors (SFRs) such as EBR-II, BN-600/800, and proposed designs like Natrium.
mcnpCode:
c Sodium (liquid, ~100°C)
c Density: 0.968 g/cm3 (use -0.968 on cell card)
m23   11023.80c  1.000000  $ Na-23
openmcCode:
import openmc

sodium = openmc.Material(name='Sodium')
sodium.set_density('g/cm3', 0.968)
sodium.add_element('Na', 1.0)
notes: Density at ~100°C. At typical SFR operating temperature (~500°C), density is ~0.856 g/cm³. Na-23 is monoisotopic.

name: Lead
description: Natural lead coolant. Used in lead-cooled fast reactors (LFRs) for its high boiling point, good neutron economy, and shielding properties.
mcnpCode:
c Lead (natural)
c Density: 11.35 g/cm3 (use -11.35 on cell card)
m24   82000.80c  1.000000  $ Pb (natural)
openmcCode:
import openmc

lead = openmc.Material(name='Lead')
lead.set_density('g/cm3', 11.35)
lead.add_element('Pb', 1.0)
notes: Density at room temperature. At LFR operating temperature (~480°C), density is ~10.52 g/cm³. Natural Pb: 1.4% Pb-204, 24.1% Pb-206, 22.1% Pb-207, 52.4% Pb-208.

name: Lead-Bismuth Eutectic
description: Lead-bismuth eutectic (44.5% Pb, 55.5% Bi by weight). Low melting point (~125°C) liquid metal coolant for fast reactors and spallation targets.
mcnpCode:
c Lead-Bismuth Eutectic (LBE)
c Density: 10.17 g/cm3 (use -10.17 on cell card)
c Weight fractions
m25   82000.80c -0.4450  $ Pb (natural)
      83209.80c -0.5550  $ Bi-209
serpentCode:
% Lead-Bismuth Eutectic (LBE, weight fractions)
mat LBE -10.17
82000.80c -0.4450  % Pb (natural)
83209.80c -0.5550  % Bi-209
openmcCode:
import openmc

lbe = openmc.Material(name='Lead-Bismuth Eutectic')
lbe.set_density('g/cm3', 10.17)
lbe.add_element('Pb', 0.4450, percent_type='wo')
lbe.add_element('Bi', 0.5550, percent_type='wo')
notes: Eutectic melting point ~125°C. Activation product Po-210 (from Bi-209 neutron capture) is a radiological concern.

name: Carbon Dioxide
description: Carbon dioxide gas at STP. Coolant in gas-cooled reactors (Magnox, AGR) and supercritical CO₂ Brayton cycle power conversion.
mcnpCode:
c Carbon Dioxide (CO2) at STP
c Density: 0.00184 g/cm3 (use -0.00184 on cell card)
m26    6000.80c  0.333333  $ C (natural)
       8016.80c  0.666667  $ O-16
serpentCode:
% Carbon Dioxide (CO2) at STP
mat CO2_gas -0.00184
6000.80c  0.333333  % C (natural)
8016.80c  0.666667  % O-16
openmcCode:
import openmc

co2 = openmc.Material(name='Carbon Dioxide')
co2.set_density('g/cm3', 0.00184)
co2.add_element('C', 0.333333)
co2.add_nuclide('O16', 0.666667)
notes: Density at STP (~20°C, 1 atm). In AGRs, CO₂ operates at ~4 MPa and ~650°C. For supercritical CO₂ cycles, density is much higher (~200–500 kg/m³).

name: Helium
description: Helium gas at STP. Coolant in high-temperature gas-cooled reactors (HTGR, VHTR) and fuel-cladding gap fill gas.
mcnpCode:
c Helium Gas (STP)
c Density: 0.000164 g/cm3 (use -0.000164 on cell card)
m27    2004.80c  1.000000  $ He-4
openmcCode:
import openmc

helium = openmc.Material(name='Helium')
helium.set_density('g/cm3', 0.000164)
helium.add_nuclide('He4', 1.0)
notes: Density at STP. HTGR helium coolant operates at ~7 MPa, where density is ~3.8 kg/m³. Also used as fill gas in fuel-cladding gaps.

name: Ordinary Concrete (Portland)
description: Standard Portland cement concrete. Primary biological shielding material in reactor facilities. Composition from PNNL-15870.
openmcCode:
import openmc

concrete = openmc.Material(name='Ordinary Concrete')
concrete.set_density('g/cm3', 2.30)
concrete.add_element('H', 0.01000, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('C', 0.00100, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('O', 0.52911, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('Na', 0.01600, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('Mg', 0.00200, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('Al', 0.03389, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('Si', 0.33700, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('K', 0.01300, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('Ca', 0.04400, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_element('Fe', 0.01400, percent_type='wo')
concrete.add_s_alpha_beta('c_H_in_H2O')
notes: Composition from PNNL-15870 Compendium. Hydrogen content is critical for neutron shielding effectiveness. Composition varies by mix design.

name: Baryte Concrete
description: High-density concrete using baryte (BaSO₄) aggregate. Enhanced gamma-ray shielding compared to ordinary concrete due to high-Z barium content.
openmcCode:
import openmc

baryte_concrete = openmc.Material(name='Baryte Concrete')
baryte_concrete.set_density('g/cm3', 3.35)
baryte_concrete.add_element('H', 0.00350, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('O', 0.31190, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('Mg', 0.00120, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('Al', 0.00440, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('Si', 0.01050, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('S', 0.10780, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('Ca', 0.05060, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('Fe', 0.04710, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_element('Ba', 0.46300, percent_type='wo')
baryte_concrete.add_s_alpha_beta('c_H_in_H2O')
notes: Higher density than ordinary concrete provides better gamma shielding. Lower hydrogen content reduces neutron shielding effectiveness compared to ordinary concrete.

name: Borated Polyethylene (5% B)
description: Polyethylene loaded with 5 wt% natural boron. Combined neutron moderation (H) and thermal neutron capture (B-10) for effective neutron shielding.
mcnpCode:
c Borated Polyethylene (5 wt% natural B)
c Density: 0.95 g/cm3 (use -0.95 on cell card)
c Weight fractions
m30    5010.80c -0.009950  $ B-10
       5011.80c -0.040050  $ B-11
       1001.80c -0.136542  $ H
       6000.80c -0.813458  $ C
mt30  poly.20t
serpentCode:
% Borated Polyethylene (5 wt% natural B)
% Weight fractions
mat borated_poly -0.95 moder poly 1001
5010.80c -0.009950  % B-10
5011.80c -0.040050  % B-11
1001.80c -0.136542  % H
6000.80c -0.813458  % C
therm poly poly.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

borated_poly = openmc.Material(name='Borated Polyethylene 5%')
borated_poly.set_density('g/cm3', 0.95)
borated_poly.add_nuclide('B10', 0.009950, percent_type='wo')
borated_poly.add_nuclide('B11', 0.040050, percent_type='wo')
borated_poly.add_element('H', 0.136542, percent_type='wo')
borated_poly.add_element('C', 0.813458, percent_type='wo')
borated_poly.add_s_alpha_beta('c_H_in_CH2')
notes: Natural boron is 19.9% B-10, 80.1% B-11. B-10 content can be increased by using enriched boron for enhanced thermal neutron capture.

name: Polyethylene
description: High-density polyethylene (HDPE). Effective neutron moderator and shield due to high hydrogen content.
mcnpCode:
c Polyethylene (CH2)
c Density: 0.93 g/cm3 (use -0.93 on cell card)
m31    1001.80c  0.666667  $ H-1
       6000.80c  0.333333  $ C (natural)
mt31  poly.20t
serpentCode:
% Polyethylene (CH2)
mat polyethylene -0.93 moder poly 1001
1001.80c  0.666667  % H-1
6000.80c  0.333333  % C (natural)
therm poly poly.20t
openmcCode:
import openmc

polyethylene = openmc.Material(name='Polyethylene')
polyethylene.set_density('g/cm3', 0.93)
polyethylene.add_nuclide('H1', 0.666667)
polyethylene.add_element('C', 0.333333)
polyethylene.add_s_alpha_beta('c_H_in_CH2')

name: B₄C (natural boron)
description: Boron carbide with natural boron (19.9% B-10). Control rod and burnable absorber material. B-10 has a large thermal neutron absorption cross section (3840 barns).
mcnpCode:
c B4C (natural boron: 19.9% B-10)
c Density: 2.52 g/cm3 (use -2.52 on cell card)
c B4C = 4 B + 1 C = 5 atoms total
m32    5010.80c  0.159200  $ B-10
       5011.80c  0.640800  $ B-11
       6000.80c  0.200000  $ C (natural)
openmcCode:
import openmc

b4c_nat = openmc.Material(name='B4C natural')
b4c_nat.set_density('g/cm3', 2.52)
b4c_nat.add_nuclide('B10', 0.159200)
b4c_nat.add_nuclide('B11', 0.640800)
b4c_nat.add_element('C', 0.200000)

name: B₄C (90% B-10 enriched)
description: Boron carbide enriched to 90% B-10. Used in control rods and shielding where maximum neutron absorption per unit volume is needed.
openmcCode:
import openmc

b4c_enr = openmc.Material(name='B4C 90% B-10')
b4c_enr.set_density('g/cm3', 2.52)
b4c_enr.add_nuclide('B10', 0.720000)
b4c_enr.add_nuclide('B11', 0.080000)
b4c_enr.add_element('C', 0.200000)

name: Ag-In-Cd Control Rod
description: Silver-indium-cadmium alloy (80-15-5 wt%). Standard control rod absorber material in PWRs, providing absorption across a wide energy range.
mcnpCode:
c Ag-In-Cd Control Rod (80/15/5 wt%)
c Density: 10.17 g/cm3 (use -10.17 on cell card)
c Weight fractions with natural elements
m34   47000.80c -0.80  $ Ag (natural)
      49000.80c -0.15  $ In (natural)
      48000.80c -0.05  $ Cd (natural)
serpentCode:
% Ag-In-Cd Control Rod (80/15/5 wt%)
mat AgInCd -10.17
47000.80c -0.80  % Ag (natural)
49000.80c -0.15  % In (natural)
48000.80c -0.05  % Cd (natural)
openmcCode:
import openmc

ag_in_cd = openmc.Material(name='Ag-In-Cd')
ag_in_cd.set_density('g/cm3', 10.17)
ag_in_cd.add_element('Ag', 0.80, percent_type='wo')
ag_in_cd.add_element('In', 0.15, percent_type='wo')
ag_in_cd.add_element('Cd', 0.05, percent_type='wo')
notes: Ag absorbs epithermal neutrons, In absorbs at 1.46 eV resonance, Cd has a strong thermal cutoff at 0.5 eV. Combined, they provide effective absorption across a wide energy range.

name: Gadolinium Oxide
description: Gadolinium oxide burnable absorber. Gd-155 and Gd-157 have the largest thermal neutron capture cross sections of any stable nuclides (~61,000 and ~254,000 barns).
openmcCode:
import openmc

gd2o3 = openmc.Material(name='Gd2O3')
gd2o3.set_density('g/cm3', 7.41)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('Gd152', 0.000800)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('Gd154', 0.008720)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('Gd155', 0.059200)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('Gd156', 0.081880)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('Gd157', 0.062600)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('Gd158', 0.099360)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('Gd160', 0.087440)
gd2o3.add_nuclide('O16', 0.600000)
notes: Gd-155 (σ_a ≈ 61,000 b) and Gd-157 (σ_a ≈ 254,000 b) are the primary absorbing isotopes. Used as a burnable poison mixed directly into UO2 fuel pellets (typically 2–10 wt% Gd2O3).

name: Hafnium
description: Hafnium metal control rod absorber. All stable Hf isotopes have significant absorption cross sections, providing long control rod lifetime without depletion concerns.
openmcCode:
import openmc

hafnium = openmc.Material(name='Hafnium')
hafnium.set_density('g/cm3', 13.31)
hafnium.add_element('Hf', 1.0)
notes: All six stable Hf isotopes have significant absorption cross sections. Successive neutron captures transmute Hf through its isotope chain, maintaining absorber effectiveness over long irradiation periods. Used in naval reactor control rods.

name: Europium Oxide
description: Europium oxide used as a burnable poison and control material. Eu-151 has a very large thermal neutron absorption cross section (~9,200 barns).
mcnpCode:
c Europium Oxide (Eu2O3)
c Density: 7.42 g/cm3 (use -7.42 on cell card)
c Eu2O3 = 2 Eu + 3 O = 5 atoms, natural Eu isotopics
m37   63151.80c  0.191240  $ Eu-151
      63153.80c  0.208760  $ Eu-153
       8016.80c  0.600000  $ O-16
openmcCode:
import openmc

eu2o3 = openmc.Material(name='Eu2O3')
eu2o3.set_density('g/cm3', 7.42)
eu2o3.add_nuclide('Eu151', 0.191240)
eu2o3.add_nuclide('Eu153', 0.208760)
eu2o3.add_nuclide('O16', 0.600000)
notes: Eu-151 (σ_a ≈ 9,200 b) is the primary absorbing isotope. Eu-152 (produced by capture on Eu-151) is radioactive and contributes to spent-fuel gamma source terms.

name: Dry Air
description: Dry air at standard temperature and pressure (20°C, 1 atm). Used for streaming calculations, room modeling, and atmospheric transport.
mcnpCode:
c Dry Air (STP)
c Density: 0.001205 g/cm3 (use -0.001205 on cell card)
c Weight fractions
m38    7014.80c -0.7553  $ N
       8016.80c -0.2318  $ O
      18000.80c -0.0129  $ Ar
openmcCode:
import openmc

air = openmc.Material(name='Dry Air')
air.set_density('g/cm3', 0.001205)
air.add_element('N', 0.7553, percent_type='wo')
air.add_element('O', 0.2318, percent_type='wo')
air.add_element('Ar', 0.0129, percent_type='wo')
notes: Density at 20°C, 1 atm. Ar-41 activation (from Ar-40 neutron capture) is a radiological concern near reactor containment.

name: Argon
description: Argon gas at STP. Cover gas in sodium-cooled fast reactors to prevent sodium-air reactions.
mcnpCode:
c Argon Gas (STP)
c Density: 0.001662 g/cm3 (use -0.001662 on cell card)
m39   18000.80c  1.000000  $ Ar (natural)
openmcCode:
import openmc

argon = openmc.Material(name='Argon')
argon.set_density('g/cm3', 0.001662)
argon.add_element('Ar', 1.0)
notes: Natural Ar is 99.6% Ar-40. Ar-41 (t½ = 1.83 h) produced by Ar-40(n,γ) is a significant activation product in reactor cover gas.

name: Nitrogen
description: Nitrogen gas at STP. Used for inerting containment and fuel handling areas.
mcnpCode:
c Nitrogen Gas (STP)
c Density: 0.001165 g/cm3 (use -0.001165 on cell card)
m40    7014.80c  1.000000  $ N-14
openmcCode:
import openmc

nitrogen = openmc.Material(name='Nitrogen')
nitrogen.set_density('g/cm3', 0.001165)
nitrogen.add_element('N', 1.0)
notes: N-14 is 99.6% of natural nitrogen. The N-14(n,p)C-14 reaction produces long-lived C-14 (t½ = 5,730 y), relevant for waste and environmental dose calculations.

name: Void / Vacuum
description: Vacuum or void region. No material is present; particles stream freely through these regions.
mcnpCode:
c Void / Vacuum
c No material card needed.
c Use material number 0 on the cell card:
c   1  0  -1    $ void cell inside surface 1
serpentCode:
% Void / Vacuum
% No material definition needed.
% Use 'void' as the material in cell definitions:
%   cell c1 0 void -s1
openmcCode:
import openmc

# Void regions need no material definition.
# Leave the Cell.fill attribute as None:
#   cell = openmc.Cell()
#   cell.region = -sphere
#   cell.fill = None  # void
notes: In MCNP, void cells use material number 0 with no density. In Serpent, use the keyword "void". In OpenMC, leave Cell.fill as None.
